# Module 1: Getting Started With Rust Projects

- Project layout
- Cargo workflow
- Compiler diagnostics
- First command-line tool

::: notes
This starts the hands-on part of the course. Move quickly from slides to the
terminal.
:::

## Module Arc

- Rust projects are Cargo projects
- Start from a tiny binary
- Build the edit-check-run loop
- Add a dependency-backed command-line interface

::: notes
Keep this module practical. The goal is confidence with the project workflow,
not a deep language tour.
:::

## By The End

- Recognize `Cargo.toml`, `Cargo.lock`, and `src/main.rs`
- Run `cargo check`, `cargo build`, and `cargo run`
- Read a simple compiler diagnostic
- Add and use a crate dependency
- Move from "hello world" to a small CLI

::: notes
These are working skills. Ask learners to keep a terminal open from the start.
:::

## Project Anatomy

```text
project-name/
├── Cargo.toml
└── src/
    └── main.rs
```

- Manifest and dependencies
- Source entry point
- Lockfile for selected versions

::: notes
Point out that each directory under `source-code/` is its own Cargo project.
Commands should normally run from inside the example directory.
:::

## Terminal: Check The Toolchain

```{.bash}
rustc --version
cargo --version
```

- `rustc` is the compiler
- `cargo` is the project workflow
- Most examples start with Cargo

::: notes
Switch to terminal. If versions are present, move on quickly; do not spend much
time on installation during the teaching flow.
:::

## Terminal: First Run

```{.bash}
cd source-code/hello-world
cargo run
```

- Build if needed
- Run the binary
- Inspect the project files after the first run

::: notes
Run before explaining the whole structure. Learners should see that a Cargo
project is executable with one command.
:::

## The Smallest Program

```rust
fn main() {
    println!("Hello, world!");
}
```

- `main` is the binary entry point
- `println!` writes to standard output
- `!` marks a macro call

::: notes
Keep this short. Avoid a macro deep dive; just name the syntax so learners are
not surprised by the exclamation mark.
:::

## Edit-Check-Run Loop

```{.bash}
cargo check
cargo build
cargo run
```

- `check` is fast feedback
- `build` produces the executable
- `run` builds and executes

::: notes
Make `cargo check` feel like a normal part of writing code, not something used
only after a mistake.
:::

## Diagnostics Are Part Of The Workflow

- Read the first error carefully
- Use the file and line number
- Look at the highlighted expression
- Apply one fix, then check again

::: notes
This is a good place for a live mistake. Remove a semicolon or misspell
`println!`, then run `cargo check`.
:::

## Terminal: Break And Repair

```{.bash}
cd source-code/hello-world
cargo check
```

- Introduce one small error
- Locate the diagnostic in the source file
- Fix only that error
- Run `cargo check` again

::: notes
Keep the error simple and local. The teaching point is diagnostic reading, not
debugging strategy.
:::

## From Program To Tool

- Scientific programs need inputs
- Command-line flags make runs repeatable
- Libraries should handle common parsing work
- This repository uses `clap`

::: notes
Connect the CLI example to scientific computing: parameters, seeds, output
paths, and reproducible command histories.
:::

## Terminal: CLI Help

```{.bash}
cd source-code/hello-clap
cargo run -- --help
```

- First `--`: pass arguments to the program
- `--help`: generated by `clap`
- Options come from Rust types

::: notes
Show the generated help before opening the code. This makes the value of the
dependency visible immediately.
:::

## Dependency In `Cargo.toml`

```toml
[dependencies]
clap = { version = "4", features = ["derive"] }
```

- Crates provide reusable functionality
- Cargo resolves and locks versions
- Applications usually keep `Cargo.lock`

::: notes
Mention that editing `Cargo.toml` is normal, but `cargo add` is useful while
learning because it gets the dependency syntax right.
:::

## Parser As A Type

```rust
#[derive(Parser, Debug)]
struct Args {
    #[arg(short, long)]
    name: String,

    #[arg(short, long, default_value_t = 1)]
    count: u32,
}
```

- A struct describes valid input
- Attributes describe command-line behavior
- The library handles parsing details

::: notes
This is an early preview of a recurring Rust pattern: use types to make valid
states and valid inputs explicit.
:::

## Terminal: Run With Options

```{.bash}
cd source-code/hello-clap
cargo run -- --name Rust
cargo run -- --name Rust --count 3 --uppercase
```

- Change inputs without editing code
- Keep command history as a record
- Treat parameters as part of the experiment

::: notes
Tie this to later modules: random seeds, output paths, grid sizes, and
benchmark settings should all be explicit.
:::

## Hands-On Sequence

1. Run `source-code/hello-world`
2. Change the printed message
3. Introduce and fix one compiler error
4. Run `source-code/hello-clap -- --help`
5. Run with several options
6. Inspect `Cargo.toml`

::: notes
This is the lab checklist. Keep participants in the terminal and editor here.
:::

## Questions

- Why prefer `cargo check` while editing?
- What makes a compiler diagnostic useful?
- When should parameters become CLI options?
- Why keep `Cargo.lock` for training examples?

::: notes
Use these prompts to close the module. Do not answer all of them on the slide.
:::

## Connection To The Next Module

- Same project structure
- Same Cargo workflow
- More attention to concrete types
- Numeric behavior becomes the focus

::: notes
Transition to scalar types and numeric basics. The next module should feel like
new content inside a familiar workflow.
:::
